We truly want you to help!\n \n For example, here's the typical lifecycle of a ticket:\n \n","newRange":{"lines":19,"startLine":63},"oldRange":{"lines":15,"startLine":63},"section":"vote of the :ref:`Steering Council <steering-council>`."},{"body":" * Please **don't** promote your own tickets to \"Ready for checkin\". You\n   may mark other people's tickets that you've reviewed as \"Ready for\n   checkin\", but you should get at minimum one other community member to\n-  review a patch that you submit.\n+  :ref:`review a patch <reviewing-patches>` that you submit.\n \n * Please **don't** reverse a decision without posting a message to the\n   `Django Forum`_ to find consensus.\n","newRange":{"lines":7,"startLine":418},"oldRange":{"lines":7,"startLine":414},"section":"the ticket database:"},{"body":" Now, report your results on the Trac ticket, and please include the regression\n test as an attachment. When someone writes a fix for the bug, they'll already\n have your test as a starting point.\n+\n+.. _reviewing-patches:\n+\n+Reviewing patches\n+=================\n+\n+A great way to learn the Django codebase is to help review patches. It allows\n+you to focus on a fixed scope of Django with a particular perspective. Plus,\n+Django tends to receive more patches than it can review which causes the\n+`review queue`_ to build up. Contributors can help Django tremendously by\n+reviewing patches.\n+\n+The `review queue`_ is defined by tickets in Trac_ that are in the \"Accepted\"\n+state with the flag \"has patch\" set and the flags \"patch needs improvement\",\n+\"needs tests\" and \"needs documentation\" unset. There is also a set of\n+`Pull Requests in GitHub with the \"no ticket\" label`_ that can be reviewed.\n+\n+Code review process\n+-------------------\n+\n+The general process to reviewing a patch for Django is as follows:\n+\n+1. Pick a patch to review. This can be from the `review queue`_ or the\n+   `Pull Requests in GitHub with the \"no ticket\" label`_. If you're new, pick\n+   a specific component to stick with. Django is a big codebase, so it's best\n+   to contribute consistently with a narrow focus and grow it slowly.\n+\n+2. Pull the branch. See the :ref:`handling-pull-requests`, including the git\n+   alias to make checking out other pull requests easier.\n+\n+3. Check the tests. If the ticket is for a bug, there should be a regression\n+   test. Confirm that the test fails if the change is reverted. Check that the\n+   tests are adequately covering the code and logic as well. See\n+   :ref:`patch-review-checklist`.\n+\n+4. Test in your own Django project. See :ref:`installing-development-version`\n+   for how. Explore the user interface and user experience with the change.\n+   Does it feel right? Can you break it? Another approach is to assume\n+   everything is wrong. Can you demonstrate to yourself that each piece is\n+   correct?\n+\n+5. Check the documentation. Is the documentation understandable? Does it\n+   provide the information the reader needs at that moment? Does it read well?\n+   Is it consistent with the documentation before and after it? See\n+   :ref:`build-documentation-locally`.\n+\n+6. Are you happy with the patch? This is fairly subjective, but that's fine.\n+   You should ask, does the approach make sense to you and is there another\n+   way to do it that's better? Also ask yourself, is the scope of the change\n+   appropriate? Are things named appropriately? Renaming things is slow and\n+   challenging in Django, so we should try to get it right from the start.\n+   Are there backwards compatibility concerns? Lastly, are there any other\n+   general concerns such as things that may worry you or that should be looked\n+   into?\n+\n+7. Review your feedback. Before you submit your feedback, consider the\n+   feedback you want to share. It takes courage to open a Pull Request, w\n... When we check if the parent has an ExtendsNode, we do:```python\nfor node in compiled_parent.nodelist:\n    if not isinstance(node, TextNode):\n        if not isinstance(node, ExtendsNode):\n            # Add blocks from parent\n            blocks = {...}\n            block_context.add_blocks(blocks)\n        break\n```\n\nThe problem is we're only examining the first non-TextNode in the parent's nodelist. If that parent itself extends another template, its first non-TextNode would be an ExtendsNode, so we skip adding its blocks. Then when we call `compiled_parent._render(context)`, if the parent has an ExtendsNode, that node will process its own parent, but the block context might not be properly initialized for the chain.\n\nThe sequence becomes: current template's blocks get added, then the parent's parent's blocks get added through the parent's ExtendsNode.render() call, but the parent template's own blocks never make it into the BlockContext. This creates a gap in the inheritance chain where intermediate template blocks are lost.\n\nActually, reconsidering the flow: when `compiled_parent._render()` executes, if the parent has its own ExtendsNode, that node's render() method runs first and adds the parent's blocks before anything else happens. So the actual sequence would be current blocks, then parent blocks, then parent_parent blocks. The pop order would reverse this—parent_parent blocks come out first with lowest priority, then parent blocks, then current blocks with highest priority, which tracks correctly.\n\nBut I need to examine how blocks are actually extracted and stored in the self.blocks dictionary to see if there's a mismatch there. _blocks({outer: [...], inner: [...]}):\n- blocks[outer] = [outer_block]\n- blocks[inner] = [inner_block]\n\nWhen rendering and encountering the outer block, I pop it to get outer_block, render its nodelist, then when hitting the inner block nested within it, I pop inner to get inner_block, render that, and push both back in reverse order.\n\nLet me reconsider the 3-level scenario more carefully. When inner.html renders, its ExtendsNode calls the parent's render method, which processes the block definitions from inner.html before rendering base.html's structure.\n\nAs middle.html renders, it registers its own blocks while also inheriting inner.html's blocks, stacking them in the blocks dictionary. When base.html finally renders, it pops from the top of each block stack—so first gets the most recently defined version from middle.html, and inner gets the innermost override from inner.html. The rendering then proceeds with these most specific implementations.\n\nLooking back at the incident description, the problem surfaces specifically when a nested block is overridden only in the middle template, not appearing in the base template at all. Let me construct a scenario to test this: base.html defines the outer structure, middle.html introduces a new nested block within an overridden section, and the innermost template tries to override that nested block.\n\nWith base.html as `1{% block first %}&{% endblock %}2`, middle.html extending it with `{% extends 'base.html' %}{% block first %}a{% block inner %}b{% endblock %}c{% endblock %}`, and inner.html as `{% extends 'middle.html' %}{% block inner %}x{% endblock %}`, the expected output should be `1axc2`.\n\nThe block registry would accumulate as: inner.html contributes `{inner: [inner_x]}`, then middle.html adds `{first: [first_abc], inner: [inner_b, inner_x]}`, and finally base.html adds to first, creating a situation where the innermost override of the nested block needs to properly replace the middle template's version.\n\nWhen rendering, popping from first yields the most recent definition, which renders as \"a\" plus the inner block's content plus \"c\". The inner block then pops to get \"x\", producing the correct result. {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01RXNEx49oKDPZqfWLAQsz5v","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"Aha! The default child_nodelists is (\"nodelist\",). This means that when get_nodes_by_type() is called on a Node, it will recursively get nodes from the \"nodelist\" attribute.\n\nFor BlockNode, the nodelist attribute contains all the nodes inside the block, including nested BlockNodes. So when we call get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode) on a template's nodelist, it will return ALL BlockNodes, including nested ones.\n\nThis is correct behavior for extracting all blocks from a template.\n\nNow, let me think about whether there's a bug in how the blocks are processed. Looking at my earlier analysis, I believe the code is correct.But let me create one more test to see if I can find the actual bug. Let me create a scenario where the bug might manifest.Actually, you know what, let me just look at the real output and see if I can manually identify the issue.Let me think about the BlockContext.add_blocks() method again. It inserts at position 0:```python\ndef add_blocks(self, blocks):\n    for name, block in blocks.items():\n        self.blocks[name].insert(0, block)\n```\n\nWait! I just realized something. When we iterate over blocks.items(), the order of iteration depends on the insertion order of the dictionary. But the blocks dictionary is built like this:```python\nself.blocks = {n.name: n for n in nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)}\n```\n\nThis is a dict comprehension that iterates over the nodes. In Python 3.7+, dictionaries maintain insertion order. So the blocks will be added in the order they appear in nodelist.get_nodes_by_type().\n\nNow, get_nodes_by_type() returns nodes in the order they're encountered during the recursive traversal. For a template like:\n```\n{% block outer %}A{% block inner %}B{% endblock %}{% endblock %}\n```\n\nThe traversal would find:\n\n\n1. The outer BlockNode first\n2. The inner BlockNode (found during traversal of outer's nodelist)\n\nSo the blocks dictionary would have {outer: ..., inner: ...} in that order. When add_blocks() iterates through this dictionary, it prepends each block to its corresponding list. This means outer gets inserted first at position 0, then inner gets inserted at position 0, reversing their relative order in the blocks dictionary. If add_blocks() is called multiple times with different block sets, each new set gets prepended in reverse order of discovery, which could cause blocks from child templates to override parent blocks incorrectly. the content from the middle template 'leaks through'. This means the middle template's block is being rendered when it shouldn't be.\n\nWhen pop() is called on a list, it retrieves from the end. So if blocks[inner] = [A, B, C], pop() returns C. But add_blocks() inserts at position 0, which reverses the order. If I add blocks sequentially with add_blocks({inner: [innermost]}), then add_blocks({inner: [middle]}), the list becomes [middle, innermost]. When pop() is called, it returns innermost, not middle. But what if the order of insertions creates a situation where the wrong block ends up at the end of the list?\n\nTracing through the render calls: when inner.html renders, it adds its blocks, then calls middle.html._render(). During that call, middle.html's ExtendsNode.render() adds middle.html's blocks to the same list. The order these blocks are added determines which one pop() retrieves.\n\nThe sequence builds up as: innermost gets added first, then middle, then base—resulting in [inner_base, inner_middle, inner_inner]. When popping, we correctly get inner_inner. I'm not spotting the issue in the logic itself, so I should probably create a more systematic test that tracks the BlockContext state throughout execution to identify where things go wrong.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":0,"cache_creation_input_tokens":308,"cache_read_input_tokens":129191,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":308},"output_tokens":5,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"5180197d-9d9f-42da-a062-97385d887e11","uuid":"843de771-a337-4ae2-b440-0f441b245305"}
